turns.content: I've been reading the docs on collections and I keep getting stuck on how to use BinaryHeap as a priority queue. I know it's a max-heap by default, but I'm not sure if there's a better way to get a min-heap without just flipping my data later.

The core reason BinaryHeap exists is that it provides O(1) access to the maximum element and efficient insertion/removal, which standard Vecs cannot do. For a max-heap, you simply pass BinaryHeap<T> directly because its internal ordering guarantees the largest item is at index zero. To get a min-heap without reversing your entire collection upfront, you use the Reverse trait by wrapping T in Reverse<T>, so the smallest element becomes the maximum of that wrapper type and sits at the head.
That makes sense for ordering but what about checking if an element exists or getting its value? I'm confused about peek versus pop, especially since I don't want to lose my data just to look at it.

The distinction is critical because BinaryHeap does not support random access; there is no get method. Peek returns a mutable reference to the top element so you can inspect or update it without removing it from the heap, whereas pop removes and discards the maximum item permanently. If you need both inspection and removal later, peek first to read the value, then call pop if you want to extract it.
Okay, I see that now but what about initializing a heap from an existing vector? Do I have to manually build it or does BinaryHeap do something special?

When you construct a BinaryHeap from a Vec using push methods, the heap property is not guaranteed until every insertion completes. However, if you call heapify on that collection after filling it, Rust will perform a single O(n) restructure operation to satisfy the binary heap invariant in linear time rather than doing n insertions.
That's helpful but I'm still confused about how exactly Reverse<T> works with generics. Can't I just use min_heap or something?

There is no separate min_heap type in the standard library; BinaryHeap handles both max and min by leveraging the generic constraints on the comparison function. By default, it uses T::cmp which orders from smallest to largest, making the largest element the root for a max-heap. When you wrap T with Reverse<T>, the internal logic effectively flips the order so that the smallest original value becomes the new maximum of the wrapper type.
